serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw French beans is approximately 100 g (about 1 cup), suitable as a snack, salad ingredient, or side vegetable.

nutrient_source.title: French beans, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for French beans, raw (cr_id F09020016). Input record confidence 0.95 with review_status reviewed. Note: the HPB entry records nutrient values consistent with dried/mature French bean composition (energy 343 kcal, protein 18.81 g, carbohydrate 64.11 g, fibre 25.2 g per 100 g), which is substantially higher than fresh raw French bean values. This is preserved as-is from the canonical HPB source. Saturated fat, sugar, and monounsaturated/polyunsaturated fat values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mature raw common beans (NDB 16028) as HPB does not report these fields; this is documented as an adapted supplement.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 29 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for dried/cooked common beans (Phaseolus vulgaris), which is the closest approved analogous food to the HPB French beans entry (which reflects dried-bean nutrient composition). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (29 × 64.11) / 100 = 18.6 per 100 g serving. For cooked French beans (fresh pod form), GI is approximately 30 per University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (18 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (1316 mg/100 g) with high dietary fibre (25.2 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: French beans (dried form) are strongly suitable for hypertension management due to their very low sodium content (18 mg/100 g) and high potassium-to-sodium ratio, consistent with DASH diet principles; avoid adding salt during preparation to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~29), high dietary fibre (25.2 g/100 g), and high protein (18.81 g/100 g) slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite high total carbohydrate in dried form (64.1 g/100 g), the exceptional fibre content and low GI (~29) significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response, making French beans suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in appropriate portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre (25.2 g/100 g), low saturated fat (0.26 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: French beans provide a favourable cardiovascular profile with negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and high potassium supporting vascular function; regular legume consumption is associated with reduced cardiovascular disease risk per evidence-based dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (25.2 g/100 g), calcium (186 mg/100 g), iron (3.4 mg/100 g), and negligible saturated fat supporting anti-inflammatory dietary patterns
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: French beans qualify under multiple anti-inflammatory dietary criteria due to high fibre, calcium, and iron content with negligible saturated fat; soak and cook thoroughly to reduce lectins and phytates that may cause digestive discomfort.

llm_training.natural_language_description: French beans (Phaseolus vulgaris), recorded here in their raw/dried form as per the Singapore HPB Foodo database, are a legume vegetable widely consumed across global cuisines. The HPB nutrient profile reflects dried mature bean composition: energy 343 kcal, protein 18.81 g, fat 2.02 g, carbohydrate 64.11 g, and dietary fibre 25.2 g per 100 g — substantially more concentrated than fresh raw French bean pods (which contain approximately 31 kcal and 7 g carbohydrate per 100 g). In their fresh pod form, French beans are bright green, crisp, and mildly sweet, commonly eaten raw in salads, blanched, stir-fried, or steamed. In dried form, they are soaked and cooked into soups, stews, and curries. Nutritionally, French beans are an excellent source of plant protein, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and folate, with very low sodium and negligible saturated fat. Their low glycemic index (~29 for cooked beans) makes them highly suitable for blood glucose management. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, they are recommended for hypertension (high potassium, low sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, high fibre), and cardiovascular disease prevention (low saturated fat, high soluble fibre). Also known as green beans, string beans, snap beans, and haricots verts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F09020016, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Supplementary fatty acid and micronutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (closest approved equivalent). GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed publication (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care).
notes: 1. The HPB source records this food as 'French beans, raw' but the nutrient profile (343 kcal, 18.81 g protein, 64.11 g carbohydrate, 25.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g) is consistent with dried/mature bean composition rather than fresh raw French bean pods. This is preserved as-is from the canonical HPB source without modification. Users should be aware that cooked French beans have approximately 22 g carbohydrate and 7 g fibre per 100 g. 2. Saturated fat (0.26 g), monounsaturated fat (0.17 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.14 g), sugar (2.1 g), and selected micronutrients (vitamin C, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 16028 (mature raw common beans) as HPB does not report these fields. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: vitamin A (RAE) data for this specific dried French bean entry is not reported in the HPB Foodo database and is not consistently available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 16028 for mature raw common beans; no reliable value from an approved source could be confirmed. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: vitamin E data for this specific dried French bean entry is not reported in the HPB Foodo database and is not consistently available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 16028 for mature raw common beans; no reliable value from an approved source could be confirmed. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in plant-based legumes per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for this raw legume. 5. The food_group in the input ('Meat and alternatives') has been corrected to 'Vegetables' as French beans are a legume vegetable; the food_subgroup is retained as legume-related. 6. No Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification applies; this is a generic international ingredient, so multilingual local-name fields are null and G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied (no mandatory language requirement for generic international foods). 7. GI of 29 is an analogous estimate based on dried/cooked common beans from the University of Sydney GI Database; GL calculated at 18.6 per 100 g serving using the dried-bean carbohydrate value.
